title: How private equity firms are disrupting transportation and ...
content:
# How Private Equity Firms Are Disrupting Transportation and Logistics

**Source:** FreightWaves | **Author:** Ashley Coker Prince | **Topic:** Webinar featuring Redwood Logistics CEO Mark Yeager

---

## Executive Summary

Private equity (PE) is transforming the transportation and logistics industry, particularly in the 3PL space, enabling companies to grow faster than ever before without going public. PE-backed M&A activity is at historic highs, reshaping the competitive landscape.

---

## Key Company Profile: Redwood Logistics

- **Annual Revenue:** $500 million
- **PE Backer:** CI Capital Partners
- **Growth Target:** Yeager initially viewed $1 billion as a "huge stretch goal" but now says "I'm not so sure anymore"
- **Focus:** Active in mergers and acquisitions (M&A)

---

## Key Insights & Quotes

### On PE's Role in Transportation

> "Private equity has been involved in our space for quite some time. It is not by any means a new phenomenon. The number of firms that are in the space now has changed. We've never seen greater activity going on from a private equity perspective, and that is creating significant impacts on the entire industry, particularly when it comes to asset-light and non-asset companies."

### On Going Public vs. PE

> "Part of the reason companies don't want to go public is because it is so expensive, on top of being a significant administrative and regulatory burden. Another reason is because there's an alternative now, and that alternative is private equity."

### On Technology as Differentiator

> "Technology will be the thing that differentiates the deals that work from the deals that flop."

---

## Market Trends

- **M&A Activity:** Logistics transactions have steadily increased over the last four years
- **Valuations:** Average transaction EBITDA multiples are now "well ahead of historic norms"
- **Growth Opportunities:** Described as "significant"

---

## Why PE is Attracted to Transportation

1. **Enormous, highly fragmented market**
2. **Absolute staple of the economy**

---

## Why Companies Pursue M&A

- Accelerate top and bottom line growth
- Build scale/critical mass
- Develop new products and services
- Access new customer and supplier relationships
- Add new talent and expertise
- Diversify away from risk

> "With the advent and convergence of technology, the ability to realize the scale benefits of M&A and get up to critical mass is becoming something that is very achievable."

---

## Challenges of M&A

- Can be **disruptive to customer relationships**
- Can be **disruptive to carrier relationships**

---

## Advice for Stakeholders

### For Customers:
- Maintain **open dialogue** with the new entity
- Provide **honest feedback**
- Establish **commitment to continuity**
- Articulate how you want to do business
- **Seriously consider** any new capabilities gained

### For Carriers:
- **Get to know the leadership** of the new entity
- **Understand** the new company's goals and priorities
- **Explore opportunities** to do more business
- Look for ways to **align and integrate**

---

## Outlook

- New companies can now **accelerate growth much quicker** than previously possible
- The competitive landscape will produce **distinct winners and losers**
- **Not all deals will work out**
- **Existing players must adapt** to stay competitive

---

*Webinar: "Company-Building: How Private Equity Firms Are Disrupting and Transforming Transportation and Logistics"*

title: Private Equity Acquirers In Food & Beverage - Hahnbeck
content:
# Private Equity Acquirers In Food & Beverage - Summary

*Source: Hahnbeck | Author: Arvin Khatibzadeh*

---

## Scope & Parameters

This analysis covers **control investments and full buyouts** (not minority investments/growth capital) in the food & beverage **consumer products** sector.

**Excluded categories:**
- Restaurant & grocery chains
- Food & beverage ingredients
- Farms and supply chain
- Vitamins and supplements (future post)
- Pet foods & pet supplements (future post)

---

## Consumer Products-Focused Private Equity Firms

### L Catterton
- **AUM:** ~$37bn (largest consumer-focused PE firm globally)
- **Founded:** 1989
- **Focus:** Middle-market consumer brands (Europe, Asia, Latin America, China)
- **Total investments:** 250+ across all consumer segments
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Viee** (Aug 2024) – Plant-based beverages, SW China
  - **Kodiak Cakes** (May 2021) – Pancake/waffle mixes, snacking products
  - **Cholula** (Apr 2019, sold 2020 to McCormick) – Hot sauce
  - **Healthy Food Holdings** (Jan 2004) – "Better-for-you" foods holding company

### MidOcean Partners
- **AUM:** $10bn+
- **Founded:** 2003
- **Focus:** Consumer and business services, enterprise values $150m-$750m+, majority ownership preferred
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Casper's Ice Cream** (Jan 2022) – FatBoy, Jolly Llama, ChurnBaby brands

### Butterfly Equity
- **AUM:** ~$4bn; invested $16bn+ in equity capital
- **Founded:** 2016 | Los Angeles
- **Focus:** Upstream & processing, industry enablers, multi-site operations, branded goods
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Health-Ade** (Jul 2025) – Kombucha (via portfolio company Generous Brands)
  - **The Duckhorn Portfolio** (Oct 2024) – Luxury wine (Duckhorn Vineyards, Decoy, Sonoma-Cutrer, Kosta Browne)
  - **Chosen Foods** (Sep 2021) – Avocado-based cooking products
  - **Pete & Gerry's Organics** (May 2021) – Organic/free-range eggs

### Brynwood Partners
- **AUM:** $2.5bn
- **Founded:** 1984 | Greenwich, CT
- **Focus:** Control investments in branded and private label manufacturing
- **Fund:** Closed 9th fund (Oct 2023) raising $750m
- **Portfolio brands:** Juicy Juice, SunnyD, Pillsbury, Hungry Jack
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Marie's Salad Dressing & Dean's Dip** (Jul 2023) – via West Madison Foods
  - **De Wafelbakkers** (Apr 2021) – Frozen pancakes, waffles, French toast
  - **Aryzta's North America Pizza Business** (Dec 2020) – via Great Kitchens Food Company
  - **Buitoni** (Jun 2020) – Pasta, sauces, cheese (from Nestlé USA)

### Kainos Capital
- **AUM:** ~$2.1bn
- **Founded:** 2012 | Dallas
- **Focus:** Food and consumer products, North America
- **Deployed:** ~$3bn across 40+ transactions
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Cremosa Foods** (Jan 2021) – Fresh/refrigerated/frozen foods (via Ferraro Foods)
  - **Whisps** (Apr 2019, exited 2024 to The Farmer Companies) – Cheese crisps
  - **Good2Grow** (Nov 2018, exited 2021 to Wind Point Partners) – Kids' beverages
  - **Ferraro Foods** (May 2018, sold Apr 2021 to Kelso & Company) – Cheese, specialty meats, Italian products

### Mayfair Equity Partners
- **AUM:** £2bn+
- **Founded:** 2014 | London
- **Focus:** Buyout investments UK and international, £50m-£500m
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Pat the Baker and Irish Pride** (Jan 2025) – Irish bakeries (via Ceres Group)

### Bansk Group
- **AUM:** $2bn+ capital under management
- **Founded:** 2019 | New York
- **Focus:** Food & beverage, beauty & personal care, consumer health, household products
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **No Man's Land Foods** (Feb 2024) – Premium meat snacks
  - **Red's All Natural** (Jan 2023) – Clean-label frozen burritos, breakfast sandwiches

### Swander Pace Capital
- **Founded:** 1996
- **Focus:** Middle-market consumer products, North America
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Inovata Foods** (Jun 2024) – Frozen entrées
  - **St-Méthode Bakery** (Dec 2023) – Fresh bread (with CDPQ and Roynat Equity Partners)
  - **Café Valley** (Sep 2019) – Croissants, cakes, muffins
  - **Fine Choice Foods** (Sep 2018) – Asian-flavored appetizers

### Entrepreneurial Equity Partners (E2P)
- **AUM:** $1.7bn+
- **Founded:** 2018 | Chicago
- **Focus:** Control investments, minimum $50m revenue, F&B sector (private label, contract manufacturing, foodservice, brands, CPG)
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Cole's Quality Foods** (Jan 2025) – Frozen garlic bread
  - **Nardone Brothers Baking Co** (Jan 2025) – Frozen breakfast products
  - **MBC Companies** (Jul 2023) – Frozen breakfast platform
  - **Marsan Foods** (Mar 2023) – Frozen/fresh foods, specialty bakery, in-flight meals

### Embassy Park Investment Company
- **Focus:** CPG industry (investor and advisor)
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Piedmont Candy Company** (Jul 2023) – Red Bird brand peppermint candy puffs, Thinful low-sugar snacks (with Sequel Holdings)

### Forward Consumer Partners
- **Founded:** 2023
- **Focus:** Lower-middle market consumer brands, $25-250m revenue
- **Key acquisitions:**
  - **Xochitl** (

title: Why private equity remains a major growth engine for DSOs
content:
# Why Private Equity Remains a Major Growth Engine for DSOs

**Source:** Becker's Dental Review
**Expert:** Dr. Barry Lyon, DDS (Dental Director for Main Street Children’s Dentistry and Orthodontics and Dental Care Alliance)

Private equity investors continue to heavily target Dental Service Organizations (DSOs) due to the industry's inherent profitability and resilience during economic downturns. 

## Historical Timeline of PE Investment in Dentistry
*   **Mid-to-late 1990s:** DSOs began forming relationships with private equity firms. Early pioneers included **Heartland Dental, Smile Brands, and Aspen Dental**.
*   **~2015:** Investment activity significantly quickened.
*   **2019–2021:** PE investment grew dramatically, becoming a major trend in healthcare services.

## Key Target Areas
Outside of eye care, dentistry is one of the most active targets for outside investment. Recently, PE investors have expanded their focus beyond general dentistry to target **specialty dental practices**, including:
*   Endodontics
*   Pediatric dentistry
*   Periodontics
*   Oral and maxillofacial surgery

## Why PE Targets DSOs
Dr. Lyon notes that PE firms energize DSO growth because they recognize dentistry as **"stable, profitable and recession proof."** 

This influx of capital allows DSOs to achieve the following:
*   Quickly acquire more practices
*   Achieve economies of scale
*   Hire more staff
*   Renovate existing infrastructure
*   Increase the utilization of the newest technologies

## Industry Concerns vs. Reality
*   **The Watchdog Perspective:** Industry watchdogs fear the PE-backed business model incentivizes profit over patient care.
*   **The Industry Perspective:** PE groups and DSOs maintain that long-term survival and growth in the industry greatly depend on **transparency and ethics**.

## Upcoming Industry Event
*   **Event:** Becker's 5th Annual Future of Dentistry Roundtable
*   **Date:** September 14-15
*   **Location:** Chicago
*   **Focus:** Emerging technologies, practice growth strategies, evolving dental care delivery, innovation, patient experience, and operational excellence.

title: Private Equity Roll-Ups Reshape Digital Health Returns
content:
# Private Equity Roll-Ups Reshaping Digital Health Returns

**Source:** Healthcare Markets & Technology | Oct 25, 2025

---

## Executive Summary

Private equity roll-ups have become the dominant exit strategy in digital health, compressing returns for early-stage investors while creating substantial value for PE firms through operational improvements, cross-selling synergies, and multiple arbitrage. This trend fundamentally challenges traditional venture capital portfolio math.

---

## The Core Problem

> "The seed investors, who took the risk when the company was just a deck and a dream, find themselves looking at a three-to-five times return on a winner, when they needed ten-to-twenty times to make the portfolio math work."

**Typical scenario:** Companies discussed in diligence as having TAMs in hundreds of billions exit at **4x revenue in all-cash deals** that "barely move the needle for the fund."

### Sectors Being Systematically Rolled Up:
- Behavioral health platforms
- Home health software
- Specialty pharmacy enablement tools
- Revenue cycle management solutions
- Patient engagement applications

**Acquisition multiples:** Typically **8-12x EBITDA**, announced in brief press releases emphasizing "strategic fit and market leadership"

---

## The PE Roll-Up Playbook

### Three Value Creation Mechanisms:

1. **Cost Synergies** - Consolidating back-office functions, eliminating duplicate overhead, negotiating better vendor terms. Example: A platform spending $2M annually on cloud infrastructure can absorb acquisitions without proportional cost increases.

2. **Revenue Synergies** - Cross-selling across combined customer base (e.g., RCM software company upselling coding automation to same hospitals).

3. **Multiple Arbitrage** - **Buy at 6-8x EBITDA, sell combined entity at 12-14x EBITDA**—creating value through aggregation alone.

### Why the Math Works in Healthcare:
- **Gross margins:** 75-80% at scale
- **Sticky customer relationships**
- Small companies lack resources for expansion or product development

---

## Evolution of PE Roll-Ups

### First Generation (mid-2000s):
- Focused on provider services, physician practice management, dental service organizations
- **Crude approach:** Buy practices, consolidate billing, drive margin expansion through standardization
- **Often struggled** due to underestimating physician autonomy and local market dynamics

### Current Generation:
- **More refined approach:** Acquired companies operate as relatively independent business units
- Consolidate only functions where scale matters
- Invest in shared technology infrastructure
- **Strategic acquisition sequencing** to build full-stack solutions

### Case Study: Behavioral Health Tech (Past 5 Years)

**Sequential acquisition pattern:**
1. Core EHR/practice management system for therapists/psychiatrists
2. Telehealth capabilities
3. Patient intake and scheduling tools
4. Billing and collections optimization
5. Clinical decision support and measurement-based care tools

**Result:** Each acquisition increases platform stickiness and upsell opportunities

---

## Structural Factors Making Digital Health "Roll-Up Territory"

### 1. Healthcare is "A Market of Markets"

> "A software company serving dermatology practices has a completely different value proposition, sales motion, and competitive landscape than one serving cardiology practices."

**Implication:** A company capturing 30% of dermatology practice management might only reach **$50M revenue**—insufficient for venture-style exit.

### 2. No Winner-Take-All Dynamics

Unlike consumer internet, healthcare software markets support **multiple viable competitors**. Hospitals use dozens of point solutions. Most digital health companies going public do so with:
- **A few hundred million in revenue**
- **Single-digit to low double-digit market shares**

### 3. Regulatory and Sales Complexity

- **Sales cycles:** 12-18 months from contact to contract
- **Implementation:** Equally long for solutions requiring integration or workflow changes
- **Result:** Substantial upfront investment before revenue materializes

---

## The Returns Reality: Detailed Math

### Hypothetical Company Journey:

| Round | Amount Raised | Post-Money Valuation |
|-------|--------------|---------------------|
| Seed | $2M | $8M |
| Series A | $8M | $30M |
| Series B | $20M | $100M |
| Series C | $40M | $250M |

**PE Acquisition Offer:** $350M in cash

### Return Analysis by Stage:

| Investor | Entry Valuation | Gross Multiple | Dilution-Adjusted Return |
|----------|----------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Series C | $250M | 1.4x | "Not spectacular, but reasonable" |
| Series B | $100M | 3.5x | "Solid" |
| Series A | $30M | ~12x | "Looks like a great outcome" |
| Seed | $8M | 44x (paper) | **15-20x (with pro rata)** or **8-12x (without)** |

### Why This Breaks Venture Math:

> "These are not the fifty to a hundred times returns that define true venture winners, the

[... summary truncated for context management ...]

title: Top 10 PE Firms in Consumer Brands
content:
# Top 10 PE Firms in Consumer Brands - Summary

**Source:** Dakota Private Markets | **Date:** January 28, 2026

## Context & Selection Methodology

Consumer brands are being reshaped by:
- Changing consumer preferences
- Digital commerce
- Demand for authenticity and experience

**Rankings are based on (criteria mentioned but not fully detailed in source).**

---

## Top 10 PE Firms

### 1. L Catterton
- **Focus:** Consumer-only investing (buyout & growth), global brand building, multi-category
- **Notable:** One of the largest dedicated consumer platforms with deep operating resources and global footprint

### 2. TSG Consumer Partners
- **Focus:** High-growth consumer, health & wellness, beauty & personal care, food & beverage, home & auto
- **Notable:** Long-tenured specialist known for founder partnerships and repeatable brand scaling

### 3. General Atlantic (Consumer)
- **Focus:** Growth investments in digitally-enabled brands, global expansion, lifestyle & retail
- **Notable:** Global scaling support, international expansion, professionalizing brands at inflection points

### 4. Advent International (Retail & Consumer)
- **Focus:** Global consumer platforms, transformation & modernization, multi-region expansion
- **Notable:** Global reach + operational improvement in categories shaped by shifting demand

### 5. Bain Capital (Consumer & Retail)
- **Focus:** Consumer goods, retail, F&B, dining & franchising, omni-channel scaling
- **Notable:** Operating toolkit across pricing, supply chain, digital commerce; strategic M&A focus

### 6. VMG Partners
- **Focus:** Growth equity for consumer brands & consumer enablement technology, wellness & lifestyle
- **Notable:** Pairs brand building with retail/distribution connectivity; supports high-growth brands earlier

### 7. Sycamore Partners
- **Focus:** Retail, consumer, distribution, complex transactions, carve-outs & restructurings
- **Notable:** Hands-on retail operations, merchandising focus, tackles complex situations

### 8. Forerunner Ventures
- **Focus:** Early-stage & growth consumer brands, digitally-native businesses, consumer tech, wellness, beauty, lifestyle
- **Notable:** Female-founded; identifies cultural shifts early; digital-first distribution
- **Key Portfolio Companies:** Glossier, The Farmer's Dog, Chime

### 9. Prelude
- **Focus:** Growth equity for mission-driven brands, digital transformation, omni-channel expansion, beauty, food, wellness
- **Notable:** Helps brands evolve from DTC to scaled omni-channel platforms
- **Key Portfolio Companies:** Summer Fridays, Westman Atelier, Banza, Sol de Janeiro (acquired by L'Occitane Group)

### 10. Roark Capital
- **Focus:** Franchise-based brands, restaurants, F&B, specialty retail, consumer services
- **Notable:** Large-scale platform consolidation in fragmented categories; roll-up investing with disciplined M&A

---

## Consumer Brands PE Trends to Watch in 2026

1. **Premiumization & brand authenticity** drive share gains in crowded categories
2. **Omni-channel execution** becomes a core capability, not an add-on
3. **Supply chain resilience & pricing discipline** separate winners from hype
4. **Retail media & performance marketing** become more operational and margin-sensitive
5. **Platform consolidation** continues in fragmented categories (beauty, wellness, specialty food)

---

## Data Source

Dakota Private Markets tracks: GP strategies, fund histories, portfolio construction, deal activity, sector tagging/heatmaps, stage focus, check sizes, co-invest networks, and private company updates.

---

*Author: Peter Harris, Investment Research Associate*
